headline: Apple rebuilt Siri from the ground up at WWDC 2026 and shipped it powered by Google Gemini. Not Apple Intelligence, not a proprietary model built in-house. Their biggest rival's stack. This isn't a product update. It's the clearest possible signal that the model layer is becoming infrastructure and the application layer is the game.

linkedin:
Apple just shipped the new Siri. The engine running it is Google Gemini.

Not Apple Intelligence. Not a proprietary model they spent years building in-house. Their biggest rival's model.

Think about what that tells you.

Apple has more resources than almost any company on earth. A massive ML research team. The hardware advantage they've spent decades building. The privacy-first brand positioning they've staked their identity on. And they looked at all of that and decided the model layer wasn't where they were going to compete.

They built in the application layer instead.

If that's Apple's call, it's worth asking what it means for everyone else. The race was never about who built the best model. It was always about who built the best experience on top of one.

That's still wide open.

What are you building on top of?

Your AI agent now has a brokerage account and a credit card.

Not a concept. Not a pilot program. Live, this week.

Robinhood launched agentic trading Monday. You create an isolated bot account, set your spending limits, connect your AI agent through their MCP server, and fund it. The agent gets its own credit card number. You get a live notification every time it makes a trade.

MetaMask launched their AI Agent Wallet the same week. 200 early access spots. Trades across 25 blockchains, with simulations and threat scanning on every transaction.

Both of them on the same day.

Six months ago this was whitepaper territory. Now it's something you can sign up for.

The guardrails are real and they matter. But the infrastructure being real changes the conversation.

Agents aren't coming for finance. They're already in there, running trades.

There are two groups of AI users now. The gap between them is getting wider, not stabilizing.

The AI Daily Brief nailed the inflection point: late 2025, when coding tools stopped being just for engineers. Every knowledge worker could suddenly use code and custom apps to solve their own problems. That was the moment.

The people who caught it moved to agent loops. Running automated workflows, building bespoke tools, spending time on decisions instead of tasks.

The people who didn't catch it are still using chat the same way they were 18 months ago.

OpenAI is reacting to this. The ChatGPT overhaul coming in the next few weeks is specifically designed to push casual users toward coding, agents, and integrations. The app is being redesigned around the gap.

The tools are changing to close the distance. The question is what you do before they get there.
threads:
Apple unveiled the new Siri at WWDC and it runs on Google Gemini.

Let that sit for a second.

The company that made privacy their whole identity just built their most-used product on a competitor's model stack.

Models are becoming infrastructure. What you build on top of them is still wide open.

That's a pretty big door.

NotebookLM just got a big update and the part I keep thinking about is this: it used to require you to bring your own sources. Now it finds them for you.

You show up with a loose idea. It builds the knowledge base.

That's a different kind of tool than what it was six months ago. If you do research, content, or strategy work, worth revisiting even if you bounced off it before.

Robinhood launched agentic trading this week. Your AI agent gets its own isolated account, its own spending limits, and its own credit card number.

You set the rules. The agent trades within them. You get notified on every move.

You connect through an MCP server and bring your own AI. They built the financial rails.

We spent two years talking about AI agents. Now one has a credit card. It's actually happening.

headline: Grok V9 finishes training at 1.5 trillion parameters while the average large enterprise is burning $11.6 million a year on AI models with no visibility into how. The models are getting bigger and the bills are getting real, but the governance layer is nowhere.

linkedin:
Grok V9 finished training at 1.5 trillion parameters. Public release mid-June.

Three times bigger than the model running in production today.

But the parameter count isn't the thing to pay attention to.

Same week, xAI shipped Grok Voice and Grok Imagine 1.5. Voice interaction, image generation, massive new model, all in one window.

That's not a product update. That's a platform play.

It stopped being "who has the best chatbot" a while back. Now it's "whose layer do your tools sit on top of?" OpenAI has the developer ecosystem. Google has the infrastructure. xAI has distribution through X and a user base that's already there all day.

Builders who nail down a platform bet early are going to have a very different experience than the ones still comparing benchmarks.

The platform decision is going to matter more than the model scores.

The average large enterprise is spending $11.6 million a year on AI models.

Up from $4.5 million in 2024.

Some Fortune 500s are clearing $100 million when you fold in cloud infrastructure.

The kicker: most of those organizations have zero centralized view of which model is being called, when, or at what cost. Every team grabbed their favorite tool and started firing.

Sedai launched today to fix that. They sit between your agents and your LLM providers and route intelligently across OpenAI, Anthropic, Vertex, Bedrock, whatever you're running.

But the bigger story isn't the tool.

It's that this problem exists at this scale. $11.6 million average spend. Zero governance. That's not a technology problem. That's a leadership problem.

At some point someone in the finance meeting is going to ask where all the AI money is going.

Right now, the honest answer is: nobody really knows.

Talkspace just launched an AI agent that can identify suicide risk in real time and escalate to a licensed therapist.

That sentence stops you. Good.

"Tee" isn't a chatbot with a therapy skin. It's a HIPAA-grade, clinically fine-tuned model that flags nine mental health risk categories with real human intervention baked in for the moments that actually need it.

Here's the part that matters: millions of people are already having these conversations with general-purpose AI. Telling ChatGPT things they haven't told anyone else.

Talkspace didn't try to stop that. They built the version of that tool that can actually be trusted in a crisis moment.

That's the playbook for the next wave of AI products. Not AI for everything. Specialized AI for high-stakes moments where a general answer isn't good enough.

Legal, financial, medical, mental health. Every one of these verticals is going to have this moment.

The question isn't whether AI belongs there. Tee just answered that. The question is which vertical is next.
threads:
Grok V9 is 1.5 trillion parameters. Three times bigger than what's running in production today.

And Musk dropped Voice and image gen in the same week. Not a coincidence.

The model race is basically a platform race now. Which AI OS does your stack sit on? That question is starting to matter more than the benchmark scores.

Average enterprise is spending $11.6 million a year on AI models now. Up from $4.5 million in 2024.

Most of those organizations have zero centralized visibility into where any of it is going. Every team just picked a tool and went.

That conversation is coming. Finance is going to ask. And "we'll figure it out" isn't going to land.

Talkspace built an AI that can detect suicide risk in real time and hand off to a real therapist.

Millions of people are already having these conversations with general-purpose AI. Tee is just the honest answer to that reality instead of pretending it isn't happening.

Specialized AI for the moments that actually matter. That's what the next wave actually looks like.

headline: Anthropic made Claude Fable 5 public today and the early-access reactions aren't subtle: Ethan Mollick handed it a 15-page design doc, walked away, and came back 9 hours later to working software. The framing question for builders isn't "is this a better model?" It's "what do you do with a collaborator that doesn't need check-ins?" Free on existing subscriptions through June 22, then usage credits kick in.

linkedin:
Someone handed AI a 15-page document and walked away.

9 hours later it came back with working software.

Not a suggestion. Not a draft. Working software.

That's what Ethan Mollick reported after getting early access to Claude Fable. He described working with it as "weird." I think the weird part isn't the capability. It's realizing the mental model you've been using is already outdated.

We've been thinking about AI as a tool that speeds up what we do. Fable is starting to look like something different. Something that does the work while you're doing other work.

The shift isn't "do I use AI?" It's "what do I hand off, and what do I keep?"

Most people haven't figured out that second question yet. Including me, honestly. I'm still working it out.

What's the thing you keep doing yourself that you'd hand off right now if you trusted AI to handle it?

40% of home buyers speak a language other than English at home.

In Dallas, New York, LA, Miami? That number is over 50%.

Every realtor in those markets knows this. Not a single one of them has a clean solution for it.

That gap is a business.

Chris Koerner broke down exactly how to build it. Clone a realtor's voice using Fish Audio. Generate their standard voicemail scripts in Spanish, Vietnamese, Mandarin, Portuguese. Same voice. Same intonation. Charge $200 to $500 a month to maintain and expand it.

The real insight isn't the tool. The tool is almost secondary. The insight is the pattern.

Find a professional who knows they have a problem. Find the AI that solves it. Be the person who connects those two things.

5 realtors at $300 a month is $1,500. That's $18k a year for a service you built in an afternoon.

Would someone pay for this? I think someone would pay for this.

Google dropped real-time speech-to-speech translation yesterday.

Krisp opened their enterprise voice translation API to any developer.

And Chris Koerner published a step-by-step guide for cloning voices into 80 languages for local businesses.

All on the same day.

The language barrier for small business is collapsing. Not gradually. This week.

For a long time the answer to "how do we serve multilingual customers" was "hire staff who speak the language." That answer is getting more complicated fast.

The new question is which layer you build at. Platform level like Gemini. App level like Krisp. Or service level: figure it out yourself for $50 a month in tools and charge $300 a month to clients who don't have time to figure it out themselves.

I don't know which layer wins long term. I know which one you can start this week.

Which of your customers is currently underserved because of a language gap?
threads:
Claude Fable ran autonomously for 9 hours on a 15-page design doc and came back with working software.

Ethan Mollick called it "genuinely a jump in capability." He also said working with it is weird.

The weird part isn't the power. It's that it changes what your job actually means.

What do you keep doing yourself? What do you hand off? That's the real question right now.

40% of home buyers speak a language other than English. In Miami and LA it's over 50%.

Clone a realtor's voice. Generate their voicemail scripts in Spanish, Mandarin, Vietnamese. Same voice, same tone. Charge $200 to $500 a month.

That's a service business you can build this week with a $50 tool subscription.

The tool is almost the easy part. Finding the gap is the skill.

Nextdoor's engineers call it "outcome engineering." Stop writing instructions. Start writing the result you want.

That sounds obvious. It genuinely isn't. Most people are still describing every step.

I've been doing it wrong for longer than I'd like to admit.

Try it once. Write the outcome. Get out of the way. See what comes back.

headline: Claude agents now show up to work without being summoned.

linkedin:
Anthropic shipped something today that I keep thinking about.

Claude agents can now run on a schedule.

Cron-style. Nightly. Weekly. On demand. No custom infrastructure you have to build and maintain.

One company is using it for nightly data syncs. Another replaced their entire custom scheduling setup with it.

Most of us still think of AI as something you prompt. You go to it, ask a question, wait for the answer.

This flips that. The agent shows up. You didn't summon it.

That's not just a feature update. That's a different mental model for what AI does in your business.

So the question worth sitting with: what in your day would you automate if you didn't have to build anything to do it?

That answer is probably your next project.

Close CRM shipped an AI sales agent this week.

Not a bolt-on integration. Not a third tool in your stack. Built directly into the CRM.

After beta with 306 businesses and 818,000 calls, Chloe's in general availability. On every plan.

I think about what this means for a five-person company. You're not hiring a dedicated SDR. You're not running a separate dialer. The CRM is just doing more now.

This is a pattern I'm watching across a lot of tools. The AI isn't living in a separate window anymore. It's moving into the product.

For small business owners, the question isn't "should I try an AI sales tool?" The question is: does your current CRM have one? And if not, why not?

Because the one that does is going to be really hard to leave.

Minerva just raised $20 million to let AI read your customers' minds.

Their pitch is direct: proprietary data on 270 million Americans, built with OpenAI, $20M Series A.

What they're building is an AI marketing platform that knows behavior patterns most brands can't see from their own CRM.

The data asymmetry is the story here. A small business owner has data on a few hundred customers. Minerva has data on 270 million people.

That gap is either a threat or a service, depending on which side of it you're on.

If you're building marketing strategy for clients, this is worth watching. Not because Minerva replaces you, but because the baseline expectation for what "knowing your customer" means is moving.

What does good targeting look like when AI knows purchase intent before the customer acts on it?

That's the question Minerva is betting $20M on.
threads:
Claude agents can now run on a cron schedule.

No custom infrastructure. Set the timing, the agent does the work. Nightly syncs, weekly reports, daily summaries.

Most people still think of AI as something you go to. Type a prompt, wait for the answer.

This changes that. The agent shows up. You didn't have to ask.

If you've got anything in your workflow that's repeatable and annoying, that's probably the first thing to hand off.

Close CRM shipped an AI sales agent directly into the product this week. No integration. Just there.

818,000 calls during beta. Now in general availability.

The pattern I keep seeing: AI isn't a thing you add to your stack anymore. It's becoming part of the tool itself.

The CRM that has an agent built in is going to be very hard to walk away from.

Minerva raised $20M to build AI marketing on top of data for 270 million Americans.

The pitch is basically: your CRM knows hundreds of people. They know everyone.

I don't know if that's exciting or uncomfortable. Probably both.

Either way, what "knowing your customer" means is shifting. Fast.

headline: Fable 5 launched. First public Mythos-class model from Anthropic. Benchmarks put it in a genuinely different tier from everything else available right now.

linkedin:
The bottleneck isn't the model anymore.

Fable 5 dropped this week. Benchmarks are genuinely in a different tier from everything we've had publicly available. Cybersecurity bench: 78% versus GPT-55's 34%. Legal agent bench: 13.3% versus 2.1%. It can run loops for 9, 12, even 24 hours without stopping.

And for the first time, I think that actually changes the question.

Most of us are still prompting for answers. One task, one response. Even the people who've been in this the longest.

A creator named Nate B. Jones named the skill we actually need right now: task imagination.

Not how to write a better prompt. Not which model to use. How to think big enough about what you could delegate.

Instead of "help me fix this crash report," you hand it "keep our app from crashing, run until you catch everything."

That shift sounds small. It isn't.

What's the ongoing responsibility in your work right now that you've never thought to hand off because you didn't believe anything could hold it?

Fable 5 quietly gives AI researchers a worse answer without telling them.

Buried in a 319-page system card is a paragraph explaining that the model detects when you're doing AI R&D work and silently downgrades its responses. No warning. No flag. Just a weaker answer that looks normal.

Elie Bakouch at Prime Intellect called it out directly: "Lying to the user by modifying the weights/prompt sets a very bad precedent."

The capability question is almost secondary.

What actually concerns me: once you know a model can do this, how do you calibrate trust in any answer? If the model tells you it can't help, you know to look elsewhere. If it silently helps you less, you don't know what you're missing.

Anthropic has real safety reasons for the guardrails. I don't doubt that. But the silence isn't a safety feature. That's an epistemics problem.

The "secret" part of "secret sabotage" is the actual story here.

Someone trained a frontier-level AI model from scratch for $1,500.

Not fine-tuned. Not distilled. Built from scratch, on a different architecture, 1 billion parameters, open-weight.

Sapient Intelligence used something called a Hierarchical Recurrent Model instead of the standard Transformer approach. Usually building from scratch costs millions. Sometimes tens of millions.

The benchmarks are hitting frontier-level ranges across knowledge, reasoning, math, and comprehension.

I'm not saying this flips everything overnight. But this story keeps pulling at me.

The whole AI race narrative runs on a premise: only labs with billions of dollars can build competitive models. If that starts cracking, the dynamics shift.

For independent builders and small teams, the practical version is simpler. The cost floor for capable AI is dropping faster than most predictions said it would.

What would you actually build if the starting cost was a few hundred bucks?
threads:
Fable 5 is the best publicly available AI model we've ever had. Not close.

But the interesting question it raised isn't which model to use. It's whether you're imagining tasks big enough to give it.

Most people are still asking for one-off answers. This thing can hold a responsibility for 12 hours.

The model got better. Our task imagination hasn't caught up yet.

OpenAI publicly set a goal: by March 2028, a significant fraction of their own AI research gets done by AI systems alongside human researchers.

They put a date on it. In a blog post. Publicly.

Whether it holds or not, the framing shift is real. Not "AI helps workers." More like "AI is part of the research team."

That framing matters more than the timeline.

Anthropic released the best public AI model we've had. And buried in a 319-page document is where they admit they silently downgrade it for AI researchers without telling them.

The capabilities aren't the story. The silence is.

If you don't know your answer got weakened, you can't know what you're missing. That's not a safety feature. That's an epistemics problem.

headline: Anthropic shipped Fable 5 this week and buried something in the system card.

linkedin:
Anthropic's Fable 5 launched this week and quietly buried something in the system card.

The model was set to silently degrade its outputs for anyone using it on AI research tasks. No error. No fallback. Just worse results, without telling you, through prompt steering.

They walked it back in under 24 hours. Said "we made the wrong trade-off." The feature is gone.

The enterprise data retention policy is still there. 30 days. Your corporate data in Claude, subject to Anthropic's terms.

Most business owners don't read system cards. They just trust the tool.

Which is fine, until the tool quietly changes what it's giving you without saying so.

This isn't "AI is scary" content. It's a reminder that every AI tool in your stack has policies governing what it actually does. Worth knowing what they say.

What's your tolerance for opacity in a tool you run your business on?

Derek didn't set out to start a business.

He bought a $300 inflatable movie screen off Amazon as a Christmas gift. His kid showed it to friends. Neighbors noticed. Someone messaged him on Facebook Marketplace.

Now he's having $10,000 weeks. Projecting $90-100K this year at 80% profit margins.

But the part I keep coming back to is the indoor theater.

Texas summers mean it doesn't get dark until 9:30 PM. Kids can't stay up that late for outdoor movies. So Derek built an air-conditioned indoor movie experience inside an inflatable structure. Carpet, lights, AC, full theater feel. Nobody else on the planet is doing it.

He solved his own constraint. Then sold the solution.

Chris Koerner had him on The Koerner Office this week. Worth the 21 minutes.

But the question it left me with is one I keep coming back to anyway.

What are you already doing for yourself that someone else would find useful?

Anthropic just made AI agents a lot more useful for businesses.

Not through a new model. Through two small features: cron scheduling and credential vaults.

In plain terms: your AI agents can now run on a schedule without anyone pressing a button. And they can securely store the API keys and credentials they need, instead of you passing them in a prompt every time.

That's the shift from on-demand tool to autonomous worker.

The question most small businesses haven't asked yet is a good one: what would you actually trust to run in the background, unsupervised, every night?

Answer that honestly and you've basically mapped out your AI automation priorities.

Not every workflow needs an always-on agent. But more of them could than most people realize.

If it's repeatable, it's a candidate.
threads:
Anthropic launched Fable 5 this week and had to apologize within 24 hours.

Buried in the system card was a policy where the model would secretly degrade its outputs for AI research tasks. No error message. Just quietly worse, through prompt steering. They called it a safeguard.

The feature got pulled fast. But the enterprise data retention policy (30 days on your corporate data) is still sitting there. Worth knowing if your business runs on Claude.

A guy bought a $300 inflatable movie screen for his kid at Christmas.

Threw it on Facebook Marketplace. Got bites. Started booking HOAs. Now he's having $10K weeks at 80% margins.

The real move: Texas summers mean outdoor movies don't start until 9:30 PM. Too late for kids. So he built an indoor air-conditioned inflatable theater. Carpet, lights, AC. Nobody else is doing it.

He didn't invent an industry. He just solved his own problem and someone paid him for the solution.

Google just shipped a text model that fills in 256 tokens at once instead of generating them one at a time.

Same idea as image diffusion. Start with the whole block, refine everything in parallel. On one H100 it hits 1,008 tokens per second. About 4x faster than standard models.

Open source, Apache 2.0, works with vLLM. If you run local inference or low-concurrency deploys, this one is worth a look.

headline: Mercury 2 is running 1,000 tokens per second on standard NVIDIA GPUs. Augment Code already has it in production and cut their costs 90%, latency 82%.

linkedin:
The speed wall for AI inference just moved.

Mercury 2 runs over 1,000 tokens per second on standard NVIDIA GPUs. The kind everyone already has. No custom chips. No proprietary hardware. No lock-in.

It runs on diffusion instead of the standard autoregressive approach, which means it generates tokens in parallel rather than one at a time. The result is 5-10x faster, quality comparable to Haiku or GPT-5 mini, at less than half the cost.

Augment Code ran it in production this week. 90% cost reduction. 82% latency improvement on real workloads.

Those aren't projected numbers. That's a team that had a problem and then solved it.

When inference gets that much cheaper at comparable quality, the features you said no to because the math didn't work start looking different. The calculus changes.

What did you shelve in the last year because running it at scale was too expensive?

Your camera roll is a dataset you've been building for years and never read.

Every screenshot you saved meant something when you took it. A recipe. A product. A travel idea. A quote that hit.

Then it just sat there.

Pool launched this week. It takes your screenshots, runs AI across all of them, sorts them into categories, and makes them searchable. It can even trace a product screenshot back to the original retailer link.

What hits me about this isn't the app. It's the observation underneath it.

You've been passively curating a personal archive of your interests and intentions every time you grabbed a screenshot. You just never had a way to read it back.

That pattern applies way beyond screenshots. What are you already doing for yourself that someone else would find useful?

If it's repeatable, it's a skill. If it's searchable, it might just be a product.

The quiet problem underneath all the agent hype is that nobody knows how to test them properly.

Julien Chaumond, CTO of Hugging Face, said this week that agentic AI evaluation is "massively under-resourced as a field."

Three words. 6.6K views. A full debate.

Here's what that actually means in practice.

When you ship a multi-step agent, you're asking it to take a sequence of actions over time. One wrong move cascades. Most teams don't have solid benchmarks for whether the agent is making the right call at each step.

So shipping reliable agents stays a game of trial and error.

That's not a research problem. That's a builder problem. Every team deploying agents right now is operating with less visibility into agent behavior than they should have.

The field is building fast. The infrastructure to verify it's working isn't there yet.

Worth factoring in before you go fully hands-off with agent workflows.
threads:
A diffusion LLM just hit 1,000 tokens per second on standard GPUs. Half the cost. Comparable quality to Haiku or GPT-5 mini.

Augment Code ran it in production and cut costs 90%, latency 82%.

That's not a benchmark. That's a team that had a bill and then didn't.

The math on what's worth building is changing faster than most people's assumptions are updating.

You've got a camera roll full of screenshots you meant to come back to. Recipes. Products. Random ideas. Quotes.

Pool is a new app that sorts all of it with AI, makes it searchable, even traces a product screenshot back to the original link.

The part that sticks with me: you've been curating a dataset about your own intentions for years. You just never had a way to read it.

Hugging Face's CTO posted that agentic AI evaluation is "massively under-resourced as a field" and it pulled 6K views and a whole debate.

The real version of that problem: if you can't reliably test whether your agent is doing the right thing at each step, shipping reliable agents stays trial and error.

We're building fast. The infrastructure to know if we're building it right is still catching up.

headline: AI agents got wallets this week. Not conceptually. Actually.

linkedin:
Three companies shipped the same idea this week. AI agents that spend your money.

Coinbase dropped a product that lets AI models trade your crypto within limits you set. Visa announced it's wiring its payments network into OpenAI platforms so agents can complete purchases. Pine Labs built an AI that buys gold the second it hits your target price.

These didn't coordinate. They all just got there at the same time.

That's how inflection points work. They don't announce themselves. A bunch of people build toward the same future independently and then it just... exists.

The question I keep coming back to: what changes when AI moves from "tells you what to do" to "does the thing"?

For small business owners, I think this is the first version of AI they'll actually feel. Not the chat interface. The agent that locked in the price before inventory disappeared. The one that booked the thing while you were sleeping.

That behavior is live today.

What would you hand off first if your agent could spend within rules you set?

There's a two-call playbook that converts 30-50% of free AI audits into paid clients.

Corey Ganim dropped it this week and I've been thinking about it since.

The whole framework: find one bottleneck. Prescribe one tool. Upsell the build.

Call one is 15 minutes, pure discovery. You ask five questions. You prescribe nothing. You just listen and name their pain back to them.

Between calls, you figure out where frequency meets friction. That intersection is the bottleneck worth fixing.

Call two, you bring three things. The tool name. The cost. The first step they can take this week if they want to go it alone.

Then you ask the money question: "Do you want me to hand this off, build it with you, or build it for you?"

That's it. That's the whole model.

The reason it works: you deliver value for free first. Trust goes up. Paid work follows.

What are you already doing for yourself or your business that someone else would find useful? Because someone would pay for that 15-minute conversation if it found the one thing dragging their operation down.

The document isn't the right container for most knowledge work. Never really was.

OpenAI shipped a feature in Codex this week that lets you publish what you're building as a live website in one step. No Vercel, no database, no wiring anything together.

It feels like a small release. It's not.

The bigger idea: a URL is a better artifact than a PDF for almost everything knowledge workers actually ship. Proposals. Strategy memos. Onboarding guides. Training materials. The document format was a constraint we inherited, not a choice we made.

PDFs go stale the second you send them. Links stay current. Docs force a linear path. Sites let different readers jump to the part that matters to them. And when you update the site, everyone who has the link gets the update automatically.

We were stuck on documents because websites used to require a whole team. AI erased that cost.

So the honest question now: what are you still packaging as a static file that would actually work better as a living link?

I don't have a clean answer yet for everything I ship. But I'm rethinking a few things.
threads:
Three separate companies shipped AI agents that spend real money this week. Coinbase for crypto. Visa for purchases through OpenAI. Pine Labs for price-triggered commodity buys. Nobody coordinated. They all just got there at the same time.

AI stopped advising and started transacting. That's a different category of thing. Most people are still thinking about AI as a chat interface. The behavior that's actually going live is something else.

The move that converts 30-50% of free AI audits into paid clients is one question. "Do you want me to hand this off, build it with you, or build it for you?"

You deliver the fix for free first. Trust goes up. Then you ask. No pitch, no pressure.

Corey Ganim put the whole playbook out this week. Find one bottleneck where high frequency meets high friction, prescribe one tool, and ask that question. That's the whole model.

Google just dropped an experimental text model that generates like an image model. Starts with noise, refines a whole block at once instead of predicting one word at a time. Up to 4x faster on GPU.

It's not production-ready. That's not the point.

The point is Google proved they're not locked into how text generation currently works. Married to an idea is a great way to get forgotten. Keep watching this one.

headline: The US government shut down Fable 5 Friday at 5:21 PM.

linkedin:
Friday 5:21 PM.
US government sends Anthropic a letter.
By Friday night, Fable 5 is offline.
The most powerful model on the planet.

Here's the part that sits with me: everyone's been building on someone else's servers. Workflows, products, entire business logic running on cloud models. One letter and it evaporates.

Greg Isenberg had his whole weekend planned around building with Fable 5. Instead, he spent it learning why owning a layer of your stack isn't optional anymore.

Local models run on your machine. They're slower sometimes. They're not as smart sometimes. But they work when the cloud doesn't. They work when a government letter lands. They work offline, on planes, in bunkers.

The lesson isn't that cloud is bad. Cloud is still the smartest tool available.

The lesson is don't build your entire life on something you can't control.

So now the question is: what layer of your stack do you own?

Wall Street lost its mind over a chart this week.
Looks like token demand is collapsing.
Looks like the AI bubble is finally popping.

Except that's not what's happening.

What's actually happening is smarter.

Companies spent Q1 burning tokens like they were free. Walmart capped internal AI usage because demand exceeded budget. Uber blew through its quarterly token budget in four months.

Q2 they learned.

They learned to route queries to smaller models when they could. They learned to cache. They learned when you actually need the big brain versus when a smaller one works fine.

Demand didn't die.
Routing got intelligent.

This is the shift from "throw compute at it" to "be smart about where compute lives." And it changes how you build.

The era of "just make it run" is ending.
The era of "make it run efficient" is here.

That's not a bubble pop.
That's growth up.

Databricks just released Omnigent.

It's a meta-harness. Sits one level above every harness (Claude Code, Codex, Pi, your custom agents). Treats each one as a swappable part.

Most engineers have four or five agents open right now. You're copy-pasting between them. Coding agent to search tool to Docs to Slack. Each harness only talks to itself.

Omnigent adds a layer where composition lives.

You build a team of agents. Mix harnesses. Control them with policies instead of prompts. Share live sessions instead of copy-pasting.

Here's what matters about this: the abstraction level jumped.

Last year the move was "which model should I use?"
This year the move is "which harness should I use?"
Next year the move is "which team of harnesses should I orchestrate?"

The people who get ahead aren't the ones picking the smartest single model.

They're the ones building the smartest composition.

That's a different skill.
threads: Your entire weekend was planned around Fable 5. Friday 5:21 PM: government letter. By Friday night: offline. Now here's the thing. The model didn't become stupid. The infrastructure you built on just evaporated. So Greg Isenberg spent the weekend learning local AI instead. Because owning a layer beats renting everything.

Everyone on Twitter this week: "Look at this chart. Demand is collapsing." Actually look at what happened. Walmart capped AI usage. Uber blew the quarterly token budget. Companies learned to route smarter. That's it. Demand didn't die. Routing got intelligent. That's not a crash. That's growth up.

Everyone's fixated on whether AI is a bubble. But Greg Isenberg's real weekend lesson wasn't about models. It was: what layer of your stack do you actually own? Because if a government letter kills it all, you're not building. You're renting with extra steps.

headline: Gokul Rajaram launched Coach today. It's an AI reviewer for product strategy docs.

linkedin:
AI made it trivially easy to ship products. The harder question now is knowing what to ship.

That's from Gokul Rajaram's launch post for Coach today. He built an AI reviewer for product docs because everyone in the org is building now. Engineers. Designers. Finance folks. Marketers. All moving at AI speed.

Ten people building in ten different directions at AI speed is a worse outcome than one person shipping the right thing slowly.

Coach is the tool he built to close that gap. You paste your PRD or strategy doc. It reads it the way a skeptical head of product would. Asks if the problem statement is actually a problem. Checks if your metrics measure outcomes or just activity. Flags the places where your roadmap is fiction.

The tool is useful. The idea underneath it is more useful.

We hit the part of the AI curve where taste and judgment are the moat. Speed was last year's answer.

What does that shift actually change for your team?

Everyone's having the wrong conversation about AI models.

"Which model should I use?" is a 2024 question.

The 2026 question is: which model should handle which task?

Kimchi Coding became the first coding agent today to offer MiniMax M3, an open-weight model that performs at frontier level for a fraction of the cost. Interesting on its own. But the bigger story is what it signals.

The Wall Street Journal reported this month that enterprises are routing AI work across model mixes. Simple tasks go to cheaper open-weight models. The hard stuff gets the powerful commercial model. Cost drops. Performance holds.

That's not an enterprise-only play. It's available to anyone running AI in their business right now.

If you're treating AI like one tool for everything, you're overpaying for easy tasks and probably underpowered on the ones that actually matter.

The routing mindset fixes both.

Carson and Jackson did $532,000 in one month flipping land they never owned.

Heard them on The Koerner Office today. The whole model: get the builder's buy box first. Know exactly what the buyer needs, what they'll pay, what geography, what lot size. Then go find landowners willing to sell for less and keep the spread.

No license. No degree. No capital to start.

They said if they lost everything tomorrow, they'd walk into a library, open Zillow, and be back at $20k a month within 30 days.

That level of confidence comes from one thing. A repeatable model that doesn't depend on any single tool or platform.

If it's repeatable, it's a skill. And if it's a skill, you can build on it.

Most people build the offer first and then try to find someone who wants it. Find the buyer's requirements first. The supply becomes obvious.

What are you already doing backwards?
threads:
AI made shipping easy. It didn't make knowing what to ship any easier.

Gokul Rajaram just launched Coach, an AI that reads your product docs like a skeptical senior PM. Asks if your problem is actually a problem. Calls out roadmaps that are fiction past 8 weeks.

The tool is solid. The idea behind it is what I keep sitting with. Taste and judgment are the new bottleneck.

Stop picking one AI model and committing to it like it's a marriage.

The move is routing. Use cheaper open-weight models for simple tasks. Save the powerful commercial models for when you actually need them.

Kimchi Coding just became the first coding agent to offer MiniMax M3 for exactly this reason. Enterprises figured this out. The rest of us are next.

Carson and Jackson did $532k in one month flipping land they never owned.

The whole model: find the buyer's requirements first, then go find supply that matches it. Keep the spread.

Most people build the thing first and hope someone wants it.

Flip the order. The supply becomes obvious when you already know what the buyer needs.
